title: AP Chemistry — Equilibrium, Kinetics & Electrochemistry
description: Advanced Placement Chemistry: thermodynamics, kinetics, acid-base equilibria, electrochemistry, gases, and periodic trends.
subject: chemistry

cards.front: Le Chatelier's principle: temperature effect on equilibrium
cards.back: Exothermic reaction: ↑ temperature → shifts left (↓ products, ↑ K decreases). Endothermic reaction: ↑ temperature → shifts right (↑ products, K increases). Temperature is the ONLY factor that changes K. Pressure/concentration changes affect Q but not K. Adding catalyst: does NOT shift equilibrium, only ↑ rate to reach equilibrium

cards.front: Reaction quotient Q vs equilibrium constant K
cards.back: Q = [products]/[reactants] using current (non-equilibrium) concentrations. K = [products]/[reactants] at equilibrium. If Q < K: reaction proceeds forward (makes more products). If Q > K: reaction proceeds reverse. If Q = K: at equilibrium. Write K expression: only aqueous and gas species included (pure solids/liquids excluded)

cards.front: Gibbs free energy: ΔG, ΔH, ΔS relationship
cards.back: ΔG = ΔH − TΔS. ΔG < 0: spontaneous. ΔG > 0: non-spontaneous. ΔG = 0: at equilibrium. ΔG° = −RT ln K. ΔG° = −nFE°cell. Entropy: ΔS > 0 (disorder increases): more moles of gas, dissolution, higher temperature. Low T: enthalpy dominates; High T: entropy dominates

cards.front: Hess's law: how to use
cards.back: Enthalpy of a reaction = sum of enthalpies of steps. If a step is reversed: flip sign of ΔH. If multiplied by n: multiply ΔH by n. ΔH is a state function (path independent). Used to calculate ΔH for reactions that cannot be measured directly. ΔH°f of elements in standard state = 0

cards.front: Bond energy and enthalpy of reaction
cards.back: ΔH°rxn = Σ(bonds broken) − Σ(bonds formed). Breaking bonds requires energy (endothermic). Forming bonds releases energy (exothermic). From reactants' bonds broken minus products' bonds formed. This method gives approximate values (actual from standard enthalpies of formation is more precise)

cards.front: Arrhenius equation: activation energy and temperature dependence
cards.back: k = Ae^(−Ea/RT). A = frequency factor (collision frequency × orientation factor). Ea = activation energy. Higher T → ↑ k → faster rate. Catalyst: provides alternative pathway with lower Ea → ↑ k without changing T. ln(k2/k1) = (Ea/R)(1/T1 − 1/T2): used to calculate Ea from two rate constants at different temperatures

cards.front: Rate laws: determination from experimental data
cards.back: Rate = k[A]^m[B]^n. Determine m, n from experiments: if doubling [A] doubles rate → m = 1 (first order in A). Doubling rate quadruples rate → m = 2 (second order). Rate unchanged → m = 0 (zero order). Overall order = m + n. Units of k depend on overall order: zero order: M·s⁻¹, first: s⁻¹, second: M⁻¹·s⁻¹

cards.front: Integrated rate laws: zero, first, and second order
cards.back: Zero order: [A]t = [A]0 − kt (linear [A] vs t). t½ = [A]0 / 2k. First order: ln[A]t = ln[A]0 − kt (linear ln[A] vs t). t½ = 0.693/k (constant). Second order: 1/[A]t = 1/[A]0 + kt (linear 1/[A] vs t). t½ = 1/(k[A]0) — depends on initial concentration

cards.front: Acid-base: pH of strong acid/base vs weak acid
cards.back: Strong acid: pH = −log[HA] (complete dissociation). Strong base: pOH = −log[OH⁻], pH = 14 − pOH. Weak acid: use ICE table, [H+] = √(Ka × C) (when simplification valid: C/Ka > 100). Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]) — for buffers. pKa + pKb = 14 (at 25°C)

cards.front: Buffer solution: how it works and Henderson-Hasselbalch
cards.back: Buffer resists pH change: weak acid + conjugate base (or weak base + conjugate acid). Add H+: reacts with A⁻ (conjugate base). Add OH⁻: reacts with HA (weak acid). pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]). Maximum buffering capacity at pH = pKa (equal amounts of weak acid and conjugate base). Buffer range: pKa ± 1

cards.front: Titration: equivalence point vs buffer region
cards.back: Buffer region: between 0 and equivalence point (weak acid + NaOH), pH = pKa when ½ equivalence point reached. Equivalence point: all weak acid converted to conjugate base → pH > 7 (conjugate base hydrolyzes). Strong acid-strong base: equivalence point at pH = 7. Indicators chosen to match pH at equivalence point

cards.front: Polyprotic acids: stepwise dissociation
cards.back: H2SO4: Ka1 = large (strong), Ka2 = 0.012. H3PO4: Ka1 = 7.5×10⁻³, Ka2 = 6.2×10⁻⁸, Ka3 = 2.14×10⁻¹³. Each Ka much smaller than previous (each step harder). For pH of amphoteric intermediate (e.g., HSO4⁻, H2PO4⁻): pH ≈ (pKa1 + pKa2)/2 approximation

cards.front: Solubility product (Ksp) and ion product Q
cards.back: Ksp = [cation]^m[anion]^n for MmXn(s) ⇌ mM^n+(aq) + nX^m−(aq). If Q < Ksp: solution is unsaturated (more dissolves). Q > Ksp: precipitate forms. Common ion effect: adding common ion ↓ solubility (shifts equilibrium). Complexation: adding ligand ↑ solubility (removes free ion, shifts right)

cards.front: Electrochemistry: standard reduction potential (E°) and cell potential
cards.back: E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode. Positive E°cell → spontaneous (galvanic/voltaic cell). Negative E°cell → non-spontaneous (electrolytic cell requires external voltage). ΔG° = −nFE°cell. Nernst equation: E = E° − (RT/nF)lnQ → at 25°C: E = E° − (0.0592/n)logQ

cards.front: Nernst equation: application to concentration cells
cards.back: Concentration cell: same half-reaction in both compartments, different concentrations. E = −(0.0592/n)log([low]/[high]). Positive E: electrons flow from low concentration to high concentration compartment (anode = lower, cathode = higher). E → 0 as concentrations equalize (equilibrium)

cards.front: Electrolysis: Faraday's laws
cards.back: First law: mass deposited ∝ charge passed (Q = It). Second law: mass deposited ∝ equivalent weight. m = (M × I × t) / (n × F). F = 96,485 C/mol electrons. m = mass (g), M = molar mass, I = current (A), t = time (s), n = number of electrons. Example: deposit Cu2+ with 2e⁻ → n = 2

cards.front: Ideal gas law and its assumptions
cards.back: PV = nRT. R = 0.0821 L·atm/mol·K or 8.314 J/mol·K. Assumptions: particles have no volume, no intermolecular forces, elastic collisions. Real gases deviate at high pressure (volume) and low temperature (attractions). Van der Waals: (P + a/V²)(V − b) = nRT. Corrections: a for attractions, b for finite volume

cards.front: Kinetic molecular theory: speed distributions
cards.back: Urms = √(3RT/M) where M = molar mass (kg/mol). Uavg = √(8RT/πM). Ump (most probable) = √(2RT/M). Ump < Uavg < Urms. Temperature ↑ → broader distribution, ↑ speeds. Heavier molecules: slower at same T. Graham's law (effusion): r1/r2 = √(M2/M1)

cards.front: Dalton's law of partial pressures
cards.back: Ptotal = P1 + P2 + P3 + ... Partial pressure: Pi = Xi × Ptotal (Xi = mole fraction). Collecting gas over water: Ptotal = Pgas + Pwater. Subtract vapor pressure of water (from table) to get Pgas. Applies to ideal gases that don't react with each other

cards.front: Phase diagrams: triple point, critical point, and supercritical fluid
cards.back: Triple point: T and P where all three phases coexist. Critical point: Tc and Pc above which distinct liquid and gas phases no longer exist → supercritical fluid (behaves as both liquid and gas solvent). Water anomaly: solid denser than liquid → negative slope of solid-liquid boundary (higher pressure → melts). CO2 critical point: 31.1°C, 73.8 atm

cards.front: Colligative properties: formulas
cards.back: Boiling point elevation: ΔTb = Kb × m × i. Freezing point depression: ΔTf = Kf × m × i. Osmotic pressure: π = MRT × i. i = van't Hoff factor (number of particles dissociated). NaCl: i ≈ 2. Glucose: i = 1. These depend on number of solute particles, not chemical identity. Osmometry: used to determine molar mass

cards.front: Electron configuration: exceptions to Aufbau principle
cards.back: Cr: [Ar]3d⁵4s¹ (not 3d⁴4s²). Cu: [Ar]3d¹⁰4s¹ (not 3d⁹4s²). Mo, Ag similarly. Reason: half-filled and completely filled d subshells have extra stability. Hund's rule: maximize spin multiplicity within subshell. Pauli exclusion: no two electrons same 4 quantum numbers

cards.front: Periodic trends: ionization energy, electronegativity, atomic radius
cards.back: Atomic radius: ↑ down group, ↓ across period (↑ nuclear charge shrinks orbitals). IE (ionization energy): ↑ across period, ↓ down group. Electronegativity: same as IE. Exceptions: IE2 of Li > Be (removing from 2s vs 2p). IE of N > O (half-filled 2p). Be/Mg: IE2 anomalies. Cs: lowest EN. F: highest EN

cards.front: Hybridization and VSEPR: molecular geometries
cards.back: 2 groups: sp, linear (180°). 3 groups sp2: trigonal planar (120°) or bent (< 120° with lone pair). 4 groups sp3: tetrahedral (109.5°), trigonal pyramidal, bent. 5 groups sp3d: trigonal bipyramidal, seesaw, T-shaped, linear. 6 groups sp3d2: octahedral, square pyramidal, square planar. Lone pairs repel more than bonding pairs

cards.front: Intermolecular forces: order of strength
cards.back: London dispersion (all molecules, weakest for small molecules) < dipole-dipole (polar molecules) < hydrogen bonding (N-H, O-H, F-H; strongest IMF) << ionic (strongest of all in solids). London forces increase with molar mass (more electrons, greater polarizability). H-bonding: explains anomalously high bp of water, HF

cards.front: Entropy (ΔS) predictions: when positive vs negative
cards.back: ΔS > 0: more moles of gas on product side, dissolution of solid in solvent, heating, phase transition solid→liquid→gas, increasing volume. ΔS < 0: reverse of above. ΔS°rxn = ΣS°products − ΣS°reactants. Absolute entropies (S°, not ΔS°f) are positive for all elements. S° is NOT zero for elements

cards.front: Third law of thermodynamics: significance
cards.back: S → 0 as T → 0 K for a perfect crystal. This allows calculation of absolute entropies (unlike enthalpy, where only differences are measurable). Standard molar entropy S° is a positive value for all substances at 298 K. Standard entropy of formation for elements in their standard state ≠ 0 (unlike ΔH°f)

cards.front: Equilibrium constant expressions: Kc vs Kp
cards.back: Kc: equilibrium in terms of molar concentrations [mol/L]. Kp: in terms of partial pressures (atm). Relationship: Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn, where Δn = moles of gaseous products − moles of gaseous reactants. Kp = Kc when Δn = 0. For liquids/solids: activity = 1 (excluded from expression)

cards.front: Electrochemical series: which metals are best reducing agents
cards.back: Strong reducing agents (easily oxidized): Li, K, Ca, Na, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe, Ni, Sn, H2, Cu, Ag, Au. Li: most negative E°red = most powerful reducing agent. Au: most positive = least reducing. The 'activity series' in metals: Li, K, Ca, Na, Mg, Al, Mn, Zn, Fe, Co, Ni, Sn, Pb, H2, Cu, Ag, Pt, Au

cards.front: Nuclear chemistry: alpha, beta, gamma radiation properties
cards.back: Alpha (α): ⁴He nucleus. Stopped by paper/skin. Dangerous if inhaled/ingested. Beta (β−): electron (n → p + e− + antineutrino). Stopped by aluminum. Beta+ (positron emission). Gamma (γ): high-energy photon. Requires lead/thick concrete shielding. Half-life: t½ = 0.693/λ. N = N0 × (½)^(t/t½)

cards.front: Spectroscopy: Beer-Lambert law
cards.back: A = εlc. A = absorbance (unitless). ε = molar absorptivity (L/mol·cm). l = path length (cm). c = concentration (mol/L). Transmittance T = I/I0; A = −log T. % T = (I/I0) × 100. Linear relationship between absorbance and concentration — used for quantitative analysis (UV-Vis spectrophotometry). Assumes monochromatic light and dilute solutions

cards.front: Acid-base theories: Arrhenius vs Brønsted-Lowry vs Lewis
cards.back: Arrhenius: acids donate H+, bases donate OH− (limited to aqueous). Brønsted-Lowry: acid = H+ donor, base = H+ acceptor (broader). Lewis: acid = electron pair acceptor, base = electron pair donor (broadest — explains coordination chemistry, no H+ required). BF3, Al3+, metal ions: Lewis acids

cards.front: Formal charge: how to calculate
cards.back: Formal charge = (valence electrons) − (lone pair electrons) − ½(bonding electrons). Structures with smaller formal charges (closer to zero) preferred. If non-zero FC: minimize magnitude and place negative FC on more electronegative atom. Used to select best Lewis structure from resonance structures

cards.front: Resonance structures: stability criteria
cards.back: More stable resonance structures: all atoms have octets, negative formal charge on more electronegative atom, smaller formal charge magnitudes. Resonance hybrid is most stable (delocalized electrons lower energy). Benzene: 6 equivalent resonance structures → 150 pm C-C bond (between single 154 pm and double 134 pm)

cards.front: Lattice energy: factors that affect it (Born-Haber cycle)
cards.back: Lattice energy = energy to separate 1 mol ionic solid → gaseous ions. ↑ lattice energy with: higher charge on ions (charge density), smaller ion size (shorter distance). NaCl: 787 kJ/mol. MgO: 3795 kJ/mol (charge 2+/2−). Born-Haber cycle: uses Hess's law to calculate lattice energy from other thermochemical data

cards.front: Crystal field theory: high spin vs low spin
cards.back: Transition metal complex with ligand field splits d orbitals (octahedral: t2g lower, eg higher by Δoct). Weak field ligands (I−, Br−, Cl−): small Δ → high spin (max unpaired electrons). Strong field ligands (CO, CN−, NO2−, NH3): large Δ → low spin (paired in t2g). Spectrochemical series: I− < Br− < Cl− < OH− < H2O < NH3 < en < CN−

cards.front: Hydrogen bonding: special cases and consequences
cards.back: H-bonding requires: H bonded to N, O, or F (high electronegativity, small size, lone pairs). Consequences: high bp/mp of water (100°C vs expected −80°C), surface tension, capillary action, DNA base pairing (A-T: 2 H-bonds, G-C: 3 H-bonds), protein secondary structure (α-helix, β-sheet), enzyme-substrate binding

cards.front: Acid strength: factors affecting Ka
cards.back: Binary acids (HX): H-X bond strength ↓ down group → stronger acid (HI > HBr > HCl > HF). Oxyacids: ↑ electronegative atoms, ↑ number of O atoms → ↑ acidity. HClO4 > HClO3 > HClO2 > HClO. Electronegativity withdraws electron density from O-H → weakens bond → easier H+ release. Resonance stabilizes conjugate base → ↑ acidity

cards.front: Quantitative analysis: dilution equation and solution preparation
cards.back: M1V1 = M2V2 (dilution). Example: 250 mL of 0.1 M HCl from 12 M stock. V1 = (0.1 × 0.250) / 12 = 0.00208 L = 2.08 mL. Add 2.08 mL of concentrated HCl to volumetric flask, dilute to 250 mL mark. Titration: n(acid) = n(base) at equivalence point for monoprotic acids. Moles = M × V

cards.front: Photoelectric effect: key relationships
cards.back: KE = hν − Φ (work function). Below threshold frequency (ν0 = Φ/h): no electrons emitted regardless of intensity. Above threshold: KE ∝ frequency (not intensity). Intensity → more electrons emitted (not more energetic). Einstein's explanation: light comes in quanta (photons) — proved particle nature of light (Nobel Prize 1921)

cards.front: de Broglie wavelength: matter waves
cards.back: λ = h/mv = h/p (h = Planck's constant, 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s). Large objects (baseball): extremely short λ, not observable. Electrons: λ comparable to atomic spacings → diffraction observable → basis of electron microscopy and quantum mechanics. Heisenberg uncertainty: Δx × Δp ≥ h/4π

cards.front: Quantum numbers: n, l, ml, ms — allowed values
cards.back: n: principal (1, 2, 3...) — energy level and size. l: azimuthal (0 to n−1) — sublevel shape. l=0:s, 1:p, 2:d, 3:f. ml: magnetic (−l to +l) — orbital orientation. ms: spin (+½ or −½). Pauli exclusion: no two electrons same 4 quantum numbers. Number of orbitals in sublevel = 2l + 1

cards.front: Chromatography and spectroscopy: common analytical techniques
cards.back: HPLC: separates mixture by interaction with stationary and mobile phase. GC: volatile compounds separated by boiling point/polarity. Mass spectrometry: m/z fragments identify molecular structure. NMR: chemical environment of H (¹H) or C (¹³C). IR: functional groups (bond stretches). UV-Vis: conjugation and chromophores. Combination GC-MS: powerful ID tool

cards.front: Reaction mechanisms: elementary steps and molecularity
cards.back: Elementary step: occurs exactly as written. Molecularity: number of species colliding. Unimolecular (first order): A → products. Bimolecular (second order): A + B → products. Rate law written directly from elementary step equation. Rate-determining step (RDS): slowest step → determines overall rate law. Intermediates: formed in one step, consumed in another

cards.front: Steady-state approximation: when applied
cards.back: When intermediate is short-lived and rapidly consumed: d[intermediate]/dt ≈ 0 → rate of formation = rate of consumption. Allows solving for [intermediate] algebraically → substitute into rate expression. Used when elementary step mechanism has reactive intermediates (carbocations, radicals, enzyme-substrate complexes — Michaelis-Menten derivation)

cards.front: Catalysis types: homogeneous, heterogeneous, and enzyme
cards.back: Homogeneous: catalyst and reactants in same phase (Acid-catalyzed esterification in solution). Heterogeneous: catalyst in different phase (Haber process: Fe catalyst, H2/N2 gas; catalytic converter: Pt). Enzyme: biological catalyst — specific, reversible, allosterically regulated. All catalysts: ↑ rate by ↓ Ea, do not appear in overall reaction, not consumed

cards.front: Le Chatelier's principle: predicting shifts in equilibrium
cards.back: System at equilibrium shifts to oppose a stress: add reactant → shifts right; remove product → shifts right; increase pressure → shifts toward fewer moles of gas; increase temperature → shifts in endothermic direction (↑ Kc if endo, ↓ Kc if exo). Catalyst: no shift, only speeds equilibrium attainment. Inert gas at constant volume: no shift.

cards.front: Electrochemical cells: Galvanic vs electrolytic; Nernst equation
cards.back: Galvanic (voltaic): spontaneous reaction, ΔG < 0, E°cell > 0. Electrolytic: non-spontaneous (driven by external voltage), ΔG > 0. Nernst equation: E = E° − (RT/nF)lnQ ≈ E° − (0.0592/n)logQ at 25°C. E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode. ΔG° = −nFE°; ΔG° = −RTlnK. Cathode = reduction; anode = oxidation (both cells).

cards.front: Acid-base: Henderson-Hasselbalch equation and buffer calculations
cards.back: pH = pKa + log([A−]/[HA]). Buffer capacity: greatest when pH ≈ pKa (equimolar acid/conjugate base). Adding strong acid: decreases [A−], increases [HA] → decreases pH. Adding strong base: opposite. Buffer pH range: pKa ± 1. Common AP buffers: acetic acid/acetate (pKa 4.75), phosphate (pKa 7.2), ammonia/ammonium (pKa 9.25).

cards.front: Colligative properties: boiling point elevation and freezing point depression formulas
cards.back: ΔTb = iKbm (boiling point elevation). ΔTf = iKfm (freezing point depression). i = van't Hoff factor (number of particles). Kb(water) = 0.512°C/m; Kf(water) = 1.86°C/m. NaCl: i = 2; CaCl2: i = 3 (theoretical). Also: osmotic pressure π = iMRT; vapor pressure lowering ΔP = Xsolute × P°solvent (Raoult's law).
